questions.hint: Read off $a=1$, $b=2$, $c=3$ from the symmetric form $a u_{xx}+2b u_{xy}+c u_{yy}$, then compute $b^2-ac$.
questions.explain: $b^2-ac = 4 - 3 = 1 > 0$, so the equation is hyperbolic — like the wave equation, with two real characteristic directions.

questions.q: Which model PDE is the canonical parabolic example?
questions.hint: Parabolic means discriminant zero; that is the case when one of the second derivatives is replaced by a first.
questions.explain: The heat equation $u_t=\Delta u$ is parabolic. Laplace is elliptic and the wave equation is hyperbolic.

questions.q: True or false: an elliptic equation has real characteristic curves.
questions.hint: Characteristic directions are roots of $a\lambda^2 - 2b\lambda + c = 0$.
questions.explain: Elliptic means $b^2-ac<0$, so the characteristic polynomial has no real roots — that is precisely why elliptic problems take Dirichlet/Neumann data on a closed boundary rather than initial data along a curve.

pde-heat-equation.title: Heat equation and the heat kernel

questions.q: What is the fundamental solution (heat kernel) of $u_t = \Delta u$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$?
questions.hint: The Gaussian variance grows linearly in $t$: $\sigma^2 = 2t$.
questions.explain: The heat kernel is the Gaussian $K_t(x)=(4\pi t)^{-n/2}\exp(-|x|^2/4t)$, the unique solution with $K_0=\delta_0$. The width $\sqrt{2t}$ shows the diffusive scaling.

questions.q: Suppose $u$ solves $u_t=\Delta u$ on a bounded domain with continuous data. The maximum principle says the maximum of $u$ is attained:
choices: On the parabolic boundary (initial time + spatial boundary)
questions.hint: Heat does not spontaneously create hot spots — it can only inherit them.
questions.explain: The parabolic maximum principle: $\max u$ is taken on $\{t=0\}\cup(\partial\Omega\times[0,T])$. Diffusion smooths and dampens, never amplifies.

questions.q: If the initial data is the delta $\delta_0$, the solution at time $t>0$ is:
questions.explain: $u(x,t)=K_t*\delta_0=K_t(x)$, a Gaussian with variance $2t$. Information from a point spreads instantly to all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ — heat has infinite propagation speed.

questions.q: On $\mathbb{R}$, the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ with data $u(x,0)=f$, $u_t(x,0)=0$ has solution:
questions.hint: d'Alembert: each datum splits into a left- and right-moving copy.
questions.explain: With zero initial velocity, $u(x,t)=\tfrac12[f(x-ct)+f(x+ct)]$ — the initial profile splits in half, each half traveling at speed $c$ in opposite directions.

questions.q: True or false: the wave equation has finite speed of propagation.
questions.hint: Compare with the heat kernel, which is everywhere positive for $t>0$.
questions.explain: Yes — the value $u(x_0,t_0)$ depends only on data inside the backward light cone $|x-x_0|\le c t_0$. Disturbances propagate at speed at most $c$.

questions.hint: In $n=2$ the disturbance leaves a tail; in $n=3$ it does not.
questions.explain: In odd $n\ge 3$ the solution at $(x_0,t_0)$ depends only on data on the sphere $|x-x_0|=ct_0$, giving sharp wavefronts. In even dimensions there is a residual tail inside the cone.

pde-laplace-equation.title: Laplace equation and the Dirichlet problem

questions.q: A function harmonic on $\Omega$ and continuous on $\overline{\Omega}$ attains its maximum:
questions.hint: Mean-value property forces $u$ at any interior point to equal the average over a surrounding sphere.
questions.explain: The strong maximum principle: a non-constant harmonic function takes its max only on the boundary. This is what makes the Dirichlet problem (prescribe $u|_{\partial\Omega}$) well-posed.

questions.q: On the unit disk, the unique harmonic extension of a continuous boundary value $g$ is given by:
questions.hint: The Poisson kernel $P_r(\theta)=(1-r^2)/(1-2r\cos\theta+r^2)$ is the answer.
questions.explain: The Poisson integral formula is the explicit solution of the Dirichlet problem on the disk. As $r\to 1$ the kernel concentrates on the boundary point — recovering $g$.

questions.q: The Neumann problem $\Delta u=0$, $\partial_\nu u=g$ on a bounded domain is solvable only when:
questions.hint: Integrate $\Delta u=0$ over $\Omega$ and use the divergence theorem.
questions.explain: By divergence, $\int_{\partial\Omega}\partial_\nu u\,dS = \int_\Omega \Delta u\,dx = 0$, so the prescribed flux must have zero mean. The solution is then unique up to an additive constant.

questions.q: A weak solution of $-\Delta u = f$ with zero Dirichlet data lives in:
questions.hint: Integration by parts moves one derivative onto the test function — so we need one weak derivative in $L^2$, plus zero trace.
questions.explain: $H^1_0(\Omega)$ is exactly the closure of $C_c^\infty(\Omega)$ in $H^1$: one weak derivative in $L^2$ and zero boundary trace. It is the natural energy space.

questions.q: The weak formulation of $-\Delta u=f$ on $H^1_0$ reads:
choices: $\int \nabla u\cdot\nabla v = \int fv$ for all $v\in H^1_0$
questions.hint: Integrate $-\Delta u\cdot v$ by parts; the boundary term vanishes because $v\in H^1_0$.
questions.explain: Integration by parts gives $-\int(\Delta u)v = \int\nabla u\cdot\nabla v$ since $v$ vanishes on $\partial\Omega$. This is the energy form $a(u,v)=\langle f,v\rangle$.

questions.q: Galerkin's method approximates the weak solution by:
choices: Solving the variational problem on a finite-dimensional subspace $V_n\subset H^1_0$
questions.hint: Pick basis functions; reduce the PDE to a linear system; let the basis grow.
questions.explain: Galerkin: choose $V_n=\mathrm{span}(\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_n)$, find $u_n\in V_n$ with $a(u_n,\phi_j)=\langle f,\phi_j\rangle$ for all $j$, and pass $n\to\infty$. This is the abstract template for finite elements.

questions.q: Lax–Milgram delivers a unique weak solution when the bilinear form $a$ is:
questions.explain: Lax–Milgram: continuous + coercive bilinear form on a Hilbert space is invertible. Symmetry is not needed — Lax–Milgram is the non-symmetric Riesz representation theorem.

questions.q: Elliptic regularity says: if $-\Delta u = f$ weakly with $f\in H^k$ on a smooth domain, then:
questions.hint: Each Laplacian eats two derivatives — running the equation backwards gains two.
questions.explain: Interior elliptic regularity: $u$ has two more weak derivatives than $f$. Bootstrapping: if $f\in C^\infty$ then $u\in C^\infty$.

questions.q: For $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ bounded and smooth, when does $H^k(\Omega)\hookrightarrow C^0(\overline{\Omega})$ hold?
questions.hint: Sobolev embedding trades $kp$ derivatives in $L^p$ for $\lfloor k - n/p\rfloor$ classical derivatives.
questions.explain: Sobolev: $H^k\hookrightarrow C^0$ when $k>n/2$ (with $p=2$). In 1D, $k=1$ suffices; in 3D one needs $k\ge 2$. This is how weak solutions become classical.
